data.query: What is the required textbook for COM SCI-X 450.46 Large Language Models?
data.answer: The required textbook is 'Hands-On Large Language Models: Language Understanding and Generation' by Jay Alammar and Maarten Grootendorst (ISBN: 1098150961).
data.llm_answer: The excerpt you provided does not list a required textbook for **COM SCI‑X 450.46 – Large Language Models**. To find the official textbook (or any other required readings), I recommend checking the course syllabus, the university’s course catalog, or the instructor’s webpage for the most up‑to‑date information. If you have access to the full syllabus or a course portal, the textbook details are usually listed there.

data.query: What are the four project tracks available for the LLM course final project?
data.answer: The final project offers four tracks to choose from: Track A (LLM Application / Mini Tool) — build a small application that uses an LLM for a specific task; Track B (Prompt Design & Evaluation) — systematically experiment with different prompts and measure their effect on LLM performance; Track C (Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering / Mini-RAG) — connect an LLM to a small document collection and build a Q&A system; Track D (Lightweight Fine-Tuning / Adaptation) — adapt a small pre-trained model on a labeled dataset for a supervised text task.
data.llm_answer:
The course groups the final‑project ideas into **four distinct “tracks.”**  When you pick a topic you first decide which track it belongs to, and the rubric (and the example ideas that are provided) are organized around those four categories:

| Track | What the track focuses on | Typical kinds of projects |
|-------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| **1. Prompt‑engineering / Prompt‑design** | Exploring how to get the most out of a frozen LLM by crafting, testing, and iterating prompts (including chain‑of‑thought, few‑shot, instruction‑style, or tool‑calling prompts). | Prompt‑based tutoring assistants, prompt‑driven data‑augmentation, prompt‑based reasoning benchmarks, etc. |
| **2. Parameter‑efficient fine‑tuning / Adaptation** | Adapting a pre‑trained model to a new domain or task with a small amount of data (e.g., LoRA, adapters, prefix‑tuning, full‑model fine‑tuning). | Fine‑tuning on a niche corpus, building a domain‑specific chatbot, low‑rank adaptation for sentiment control, etc. |
| **3. Retrieval‑augmented / Knowledge‑grounded generation** | Combining an LLM with an external knowledge source (vector store, search engine, database, APIs) so that the model can retrieve factual information at inference time. | RAG‑based question answering, code‑search assistants, document‑grounded summarisation, tool‑calling agents, etc. |
| **4. Evaluation, benchmarking, or safety‑focused studies** | Designing experiments to measure model behaviour, robustness, bias, or alignment; building new metrics or test suites; or conducting systematic ablation studies. | Prompt‑robustness benchmarks, bias/fairness audits, hallucination detection, human‑in‑the‑loop evaluation, etc. |

When you submit your final‑project proposal you’ll indicate which of these four tracks you’re pursuing, and the project rubric (problem statement, system description, results, demo, and “lessons learned”) is applied consistently across all tracks.

data.query: What topics are covered in Week 3 of the course?
data.answer: Week 3 covers fine-tuning and adaptation of LLMs to tailor them towards particular NLP objectives, using sentiment analysis (binary classification) of movie reviews as the running example. Topics include methods for LLM adaptation, traditional classification with machine learning and deep learning, using pretrained LLMs for classification, and fine-tuning LLMs for classification. Students also review an article on LLM adaptation and risks of misalignment, and read Chapters 4 and 11 of the textbook.
data.llm_answer:
### Week 3 – “Getting Your Model Up and Running” During the third week the course moves from theory to hands‑on practice. The focus is on **loading, configuring, and managing large language models (LLMs) on a GPU‑enabled workstation**. The main topics covered are: | # | Topic | What you’ll learn / do | |---|-------|------------------------| | 1 | **Using the 🤗 Transformers `pipeline` API** | • Load a pre‑trained model and its tokenizer in one call.
• Set `return_all_scores=True` to get the full probability distribution for classification tasks.
• Choose the device (`cpu`, `cuda:0`, etc.) and verify that the model is really on the GPU. | | 2 | **Understanding tokenizers** | • How a tokenizer converts raw text into token IDs.
• Visualising the tokenisation process (as shown in Figure 4‑6 of the textbook).
• Differences between word‑piece, BPE, and sentence‑piece tokenisers. | | 3 | **Memory management for large models** | • Explicitly delete objects (`del model, tokenizer, pipe`).
• Run Python’s garbage collector (`gc.collect()`).
• Free GPU memory with `torch.cuda.empty_cache()`.
• Why this is essential when you switch from one model to another in the same notebook. | | 4 | **Loading a second model – Phi‑3** | • Pull the model from a Hugging‑Face repo using `repo_id` and `filename`.
• Set `n_gpu_layers = -1` to run **all** layers on the GPU (vs. CPU‑offload).
• Adjust the context window (`n_ctx`) to match the model’s maximum sequence length. | | 5 | **Configuring model‑specific parameters** | • Understanding the meaning of `n_gpu_layers`, `n_ctx`, `repo_id`, and `filename`.
• How to read a model’s `config.json` to discover the correct values.
• Practical tips for troubleshooting “out‑of‑memory” errors. | | 6 | **Best practices for clean, reproducible notebooks** | • Adding markdown cells that explain each step.
• Commenting code blocks so future readers (or you) can quickly grasp the purpose.
• Keeping the notebook tidy – e.g., grouping imports, resetting the runtime, and documenting any “gotchas”. | | 7 | **Debugging common pitfalls** | • Why a model may load but produce nonsensical results (e.g., mismatched tokenizer, wrong `return_all_scores` flag).
• Strategies for verifying that the correct model is being used (checking `model.config`, printing a few token IDs, etc.). | | 8 | **Thinking outside the box** | • Encouraging you to experiment with swapping datasets, changing hyper‑parameters, or chaining multiple pipelines together rather than just copying‑and‑pasting code. | --- #### How the topics fit together 1. **Start with a simple sentiment‑analysis pipeline** (the `cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest` model). 2. **Inspect the tokenizer** to see exactly how the input text is broken down. 3. **Clear the GPU** so you can safely load a second, larger model (Phi‑3). 4. **Load Phi‑3 with the right flags** (`n_gpu_layers`, `n_ctx`) and verify that it runs entirely on the GPU. 5. **Wrap everything in clean, well‑documented notebook cells** so the workflow can be reproduced by classmates or in future projects. By the end of Week 3 you should be comfortable: * Switching between different LLMs in the same environment. * Managing GPU memory manually to avoid crashes. * Understanding the role of tokenizers and model configuration parameters. * Writing clear, maintainable notebook code that other students can follow. These skills lay the groundwork for the more advanced topics that follow in Weeks 4 and 5 (e.g., fine‑tuning, prompt engineering, and building end‑to‑end applications).

data.query: What is Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and what week does the course cover it?
data.answer: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is a technique that grounds LLM responses in external, verifiable data, helping to reduce hallucinations and allowing models to use private or up-to-date information. It is covered in Week 5, along with semantic search and implementing RAG with Python. The week uses Chapter 8 of the textbook as the primary reading.
data.llm_answer:
**Retrieval‑Augmented Generation (RAG)**  

Retrieval‑Augmented Generation is a hybrid approach that couples two components:

1. **Retriever** – a search or embedding‑based module that looks up the most relevant pieces of external knowledge (e.g., documents, passages, or database entries) for a given query.  
2. **Generator** – a large language model (LLM) that takes the retrieved text as additional context and then produces its answer.

By feeding the generator with up‑to‑date, factual information that it has actually “seen” at inference time, RAG improves the factual correctness, grounding, and overall relevance of the model’s output. Typical RAG pipelines also include post‑processing steps such as query rewriting, passage re‑ranking, or the use of soft‑negative examples to make the retriever more robust.

---

### Which week of the course covers RAG?

The excerpt you provided does not contain the weekly schedule of the course, so the specific week in which RAG is taught isn’t mentioned in the material we have. If you have access to the full syllabus or a calendar view, you can look for a module titled “Retrieval‑Augmented Generation,” “RAG,” or “Advanced Retrieval Techniques” to find the exact week. If you share that part of the syllabus, I can point out the week for you.

data.query: What are the three types of prompting strategies discussed in the course's prompt engineering module?
data.answer: The prompt engineering module (Week 4) covers three prompting strategies: zero-shot prompting (asking the model without any examples), few-shot prompting (providing a small number of input-output examples), and chain-of-thought prompting (guiding the model to reason step by step before producing a final answer). The week also covers how to use these techniques with Python and LangChain.
data.llm_answer:
The prompt‑engineering module groups the techniques into three main “prompting strategies”:

| Prompting strategy | What it means | Typical use case |
|--------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Chain prompting** (also called *chain‑of‑thought* or *breaking up the problem*) | The original task is split into a sequence of smaller sub‑questions or steps, and the model is asked to solve each step in order. The final answer is produced by aggregating the intermediate results. | Complex reasoning, multi‑step calculations, planning, or any problem that benefits from explicit decomposition. |
| **In‑context learning** (providing examples) | A few representative input‑output pairs are placed in the prompt as demonstrations. The model infers the pattern from those examples and applies it to the new query. | Tasks where you can show a handful of “shots” (few‑shot learning), such as classification, transformation, or formatting tasks. |
| **Instruction‑based prompting** | The prompt is phrased as a clear, concise instruction (e.g., “Summarize the following paragraph in two sentences”). No explicit examples are given; the model follows the directive directly. | Straight‑forward commands, summarization, translation, question answering, or any scenario where a single, well‑written instruction is sufficient. |

These three strategies—**chain prompting**, **in‑context learning**, and **instruction‑based prompting**—are the core approaches the course highlights for getting reliable, high‑quality outputs from large language models. (A fourth topic, *output verification*, is covered separately as a post‑generation quality‑check, not as a prompting strategy.)

data.query: What are the required deliverables for the LLM course final project?
data.answer: The final project has four required deliverables: (1) a Project Proposal (~1 page PDF) due May 18, including the chosen track, task description, planned models and libraries, data source and size, evaluation plan, and a backup plan; (2) a Final Report (~4–6 pages PDF) structured around introduction, data and setup, methods, experiments and results, qualitative analysis, ethics and limitations, and conclusion; (3) Code & Artifacts as a zip file containing main scripts, a README, any datasets, and readable organized code; (4) a Short Presentation (5–10 minutes) covering problem motivation, what was built, key results, a demo, and lessons learned.
data.llm_answer:
The excerpt you shared (which covers tokenization, embeddings, and a short code snippet for loading a playlist dataset) doesn’t contain the section that lists the final‑project deliverables for the LLM course.  

**What you can do next**

| Option | How to proceed |
|--------|----------------|
| **Check the course syllabus or Canvas/learning‑management system** | Most courses list the final‑project requirements (e.g., a written report, code repository, demo video, presentation slides) in the syllabus or a dedicated “Assignments” page. |
| **Look for a “Final Project” or “Capstone” module in the course materials** | The PDF you’re reading may have a later chapter or appendix that details the deliverables. Try searching the PDF for keywords such as “final project,” “deliverables,” “submission,” or “assessment.” |
| **Ask the instructor or teaching assistant** | If the information isn’t publicly posted, a quick email or forum post to the course staff will get you the exact list. |
| **Provide more of the course document** | If you can share the portion of the PDF that discusses the project (or the table of contents), I can extract the required items for you. |

**Typical deliverables for an LLM‑focused final project (just as a reference)**  

While I can’t confirm the exact list for your specific class, many LLM courses ask students to submit:

1. **Project proposal** (problem statement, objectives, dataset, methodology) – often due early in the term.  
2. **Code repository** (GitHub/GitLab) with a clear README, data preprocessing scripts, model training/evaluation code, and instructions to reproduce results.  
3. **Technical report** (8‑12 pages) covering background, approach, experiments, results, analysis, and discussion of limitations.  
4. **Demo or notebook** (e.g., a Colab notebook or Streamlit app) that lets reviewers interact with the trained model.  
5. **Presentation slides** (10‑15 minutes) for the final live or recorded presentation.  
6. **Reflection or future‑work section** (sometimes a short paragraph) describing what you would improve next.

If any of these sound familiar, they’re likely part of the required deliverables, but you’ll need to verify the exact list for your course. Let me know if you can locate the relevant page or need help interpreting any of the requirements once you have them!

data.query: What model optimization techniques are covered in Week 8 of the course?
data.answer: Week 8 covers optimization and approximation techniques aimed at reducing the massive computational demands of LLM use. Topics include parameter-efficient fine-tuning, distillation, quantization, and pruning. The week examines the motivations behind these approaches and the trade-offs between model size, inference speed, and accuracy. Students finish reading Chapter 12 of the textbook and complete an assignment on evaluation and approximation.
data.llm_answer:
**Week 8 – Model‑Optimization Techniques** During the eighth week the course walks through a toolbox of practical tricks you can use to make large language models run faster, cheaper, and more reliably. The main techniques that are covered are: | Category | Specific technique(s) | What it does | |----------|-----------------------|--------------| | **Hardware‑level tuning** | • `n_gpu_layers = -1` (run *all* layers on the GPU)
• `n_ctx` (adjust the model’s context window)
• Explicit memory clean‑up (`gc.collect()`, `torch.cuda.empty_cache()`) | Forces the whole model onto the GPU, lets you trade‑off context length vs. memory, and frees up GPU RAM between experiments. | | **Model selection & sizing** | • Choosing a task‑specific model (e.g., Falcon, Phi‑3)
• “Choosing a Text‑Generation Model” (criteria such as parameter count, quantisation, latency) | Helps you pick the smallest model that still meets accuracy requirements, reducing inference cost. | | **Quantisation & layer‑wise off‑loading** | Implicitly discussed when setting `n_gpu_layers` (you can keep some layers on CPU or use 4‑bit/8‑bit quantisation). | Lowers memory footprint and speeds up inference without a large loss in quality. | | **Prompt‑engineering & task‑specific fine‑tuning** | “Using a Task‑Specific Model” and “feature extraction step” | Fine‑tune or adapt a pretrained encoder‑only/encoder‑decoder model to the exact downstream task, which often yields higher accuracy with fewer inference steps. | | **Efficient retrieval / vector search** | FAISS, “Nearest‑neighbor search versus vector databases” | Pre‑compute embeddings and use an optimized similarity‑search library (FAISS) to avoid running the full model for every query. | | **Robust API / service handling** | Exponential back‑off | When calling remote inference endpoints (e.g., OpenAI, Hugging Face Inference API), automatically retry with increasing delays to avoid throttling and improve overall throughput. | | **Evaluation‑driven optimisation** | F1‑score, confusion matrices, “validating output / Output Verification” | Use quantitative metrics to identify bottlenecks (e.g., too many false positives) and then iterate on model size, prompt, or post‑processing to improve performance. | | **Pipeline‑level tricks** | `return_all_scores=True` (batch scoring), tokeniser‑based preprocessing | Reduce the number of forward passes by scoring all classes at once and by feeding the model already‑tokenised inputs. | ### TL;DR Week 8 teaches you how to **speed up and shrink inference** by: 1. **Running the whole model on the GPU** (or selectively off‑loading layers) and tuning the context size. 2. **Cleaning GPU memory** between runs. 3. **Choosing the right model size** (Falcon, Phi‑3, task‑specific fine‑tuned variants). 4. **Applying quantisation / layer‑wise off‑loading**. 5. **Using FAISS for fast vector‑search retrieval** instead of re‑running the model. 6. **Employing exponential back‑off** for reliable API calls. 7. **Guided optimisation via metrics** (F1, confusion matrix) and **feature‑extraction** pipelines. These techniques together give you a systematic way to get the best trade‑off between latency, cost, and accuracy for any LLM‑based application.

data.query: What evaluation topics and frameworks does the course cover for assessing LLM performance?
data.answer: Week 7 explores model capacity, evaluation, and benchmarking to help determine which LLMs are best suited for a given use case. Topics include capacity and efficiency, evaluation metrics, and benchmark suites. Students read parts of Chapter 12 of the textbook and two online articles about evaluation metrics, and complete an assignment on evaluation. The week also includes a discussion post responding to a reading about benchmarking LLMs.
data.llm_answer:
**Evaluation topics covered in the course**

| Topic | What you’ll learn |
|-------|-------------------|
| **Quantitative performance metrics** | How to compute and interpret **BLEU** and **ROUGE** scores for generation quality, and how to use them to compare models or fine‑tuned variants. |
| **Qualitative risk assessment** | Techniques for spotting and mitigating **bias**, **hallucination**, and **misuse** in LLM outputs, including prompt‑engineering checks and post‑processing filters. |
| **End‑to‑end evaluation pipelines** | Building reproducible evaluation scripts that combine automatic metrics with human‑in‑the‑loop review (e.g., crowdsourced rating, expert annotation). |
| **Benchmarking on downstream tasks** | Applying the above metrics to specific use‑cases such as retrieval‑augmented generation, question answering, and conversational agents. |

**Frameworks and toolkits used for evaluation**

| Framework / Library | Role in the course |
|---------------------|--------------------|
| **Hugging Face 🤗 Transformers & Datasets** | Loading pre‑trained models, tokenizers, and benchmark datasets; running inference and computing BLEU/ROUGE via `evaluate` or `datasets` utilities. |
| **LangChain** | Orchestrating LLM calls, chaining prompts, and integrating evaluation hooks (e.g., logging metric results after each chain step). |
| **OpenAI API** | Accessing proprietary models for comparative evaluation and learning how to capture usage metrics (latency, token cost) alongside quality scores. |
| **ReAct** (Reason+Act) | Designing agentic workflows that can self‑evaluate their reasoning steps; measuring correctness of intermediate actions. |
| **AutoGPT** | Setting up autonomous agents that generate self‑evaluation reports, useful for large‑scale benchmarking and error analysis. |

Together, these topics and frameworks give you a complete toolbox for **measuring, interpreting, and improving** the performance of large language models in both research and production settings.

data.query: What Python packages and tools will be used throughout the LLM course?
data.answer: The course makes extensive use of Python and several key tools. Week 1 introduces the Hugging Face transformers library (for open-source models) and the OpenAI API (for proprietary models). Week 4 introduces LangChain for building agentic LLM systems with prompt engineering pipelines. Week 6 further covers LangChain in the context of agentic LLMs, ReAct, AutoGPT, and MCP. PyTorch is used when implementing transformer models from scratch. Students also set up accounts for JupyterHub, ChatGPT, OpenAI, and Hugging Face in Week 1.
data.llm_answer:
Below is a concise “shopping‑list” of the Python packages, libraries and auxiliary tools that appear (or are implied) in the notebooks and scripts used throughout the **LLM Foundations** course.  
You’ll see them referenced in the code snippets, in the model‑loading instructions, and in the data‑pre‑processing steps.

| Category | Package / Tool | What it’s used for in the course | Typical import statement |
|----------|----------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Core Python / Std‑lib** | `random` | Simple random sampling (e.g., shuffling, picking soft‑negatives). | `import random` |
| | `gc` (garbage collector) | Explicitly free Python objects and release memory before loading a new model. | `import gc` |
| | `os`, `sys` (occasionally) | Path handling, environment configuration, printing device info. | `import os, sys` |
| **Deep‑Learning Framework** | **`torch`** (PyTorch) | Tensor computations, moving models to GPU, clearing CUDA cache (`torch.cuda.empty_cache()`), and sometimes building custom loss functions. | `import torch` |
| **Model & Tokenizer Management** | **`transformers`** (Hugging‑Face) – the `pipeline` API | Loads pre‑trained LLMs (e.g., `cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest`, Phi‑3), provides tokenizer, handles inference (`return_all_scores=True`). | `from transformers import pipeline` |
| | **`huggingface_hub`** (implicitly) | Pulls model files from a HF repo (`repo_id`, `filename`). Usually invoked under the hood by `transformers`. | `from huggingface_hub import hf_hub_download` (optional) |
| **Dataset Handling** | **`datasets`** (🤗 Datasets) | Downloads, streams, and manipulates benchmark sets such as GLUE‑MNLI; provides `Dataset` objects, `load_dataset`, column removal, filtering, etc. | `from datasets import load_dataset, Dataset` |
| **Progress / Logging** | **`tqdm`** | Gives a nice progress bar for loops (e.g., iterating over the training set to generate soft‑negatives). | `from tqdm import tqdm` |
| **GPU / Hardware Utilities** | **CUDA** (via PyTorch) | Executes all model layers on the GPU (`device="cuda:0"`), clears GPU memory (`torch.cuda.empty_cache()`). No separate import – accessed through `torch`. |
| **Model‑specific wrappers (optional, shown in the “Phi‑3” section)** | **`llama_cpp`** or **`ctransformers`** (depending on the instructor’s choice) | When loading lightweight GGUF‑format models (e.g., Phi‑3) you may see `n_gpu_layers`, `n_ctx`, `repo_id`, `filename` arguments. These flags belong to the C‑transformers/llama‑cpp Python bindings. | `from llama_cpp import Llama` or `from ctransformers import AutoModelForCausalLM` |
| **Environment / Dependency Management** | **`conda`** or **`pip`** | Installing the above packages (e.g., `conda install pytorch torchvision torchaudio cudatoolkit=11.8 -c pytorch`, `pip install transformers datasets tqdm`). Not a code import but essential for reproducibility. |
| **Optional utilities that appear in later notebooks** | **`numpy`**, **`pandas`**, **`scikit-learn`** | Numerical ops, data‑frame handling, metric calculations (accuracy, F1, etc.). | `import numpy as np`, `import pandas as pd`, `from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score` |
| | **`accelerate`** (HF Accelerate) | Simplifies multi‑GPU / mixed‑precision training; sometimes used when scaling up from the small demo runs. | `from accelerate import Accelerator` |

---

### How the pieces fit together in a typical workflow

1. **Setup & Imports**  
   ```python
   import gc, random
   import torch
   from tqdm import tqdm
   from datasets import load_dataset, Dataset
   from transformers import pipeline   # or AutoModelForCausalLM + AutoTokenizer
   ```

2. **Load a model & tokenizer** (via `pipeline` or the lower‑level API)  
   ```python
   pipe = pipeline(
       model="cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest",
       tokenizer="cardiffnlp/twitter-roberta-base-sentiment-latest",
       return_all_scores=True,
       device="cuda:0"
   )
   ```

3. **Free memory before swapping models**  
   ```python
   del pipe, model, tokenizer
   gc.collect()
   torch.cuda.empty_cache()
   ```

4. **Load a second (larger) model – e.g., Phi‑3**  
   ```python
   # Example using ctransformers (the exact call may vary)
   from ctransformers import AutoModelForCausalLM
   phi3 = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
       repo_id="microsoft/Phi-3-mini-4k-instruct",
       filename="phi3-mini-4k.gguf",
       n_gpu_layers=-1,   # run all layers on GPU
       n_ctx=4096
   )
   ```

5. **Prepare data** (GLUE‑MNLI in the snippet)  
   ```python
   mnli = load_dataset("glue", "mnli", split="train").select(range(50_000))
   mnli = mnli.remove_columns("idx")
   mnli = mnli.filter(lambda x: x["label"] == 0)   # keep only “contradiction” examples
   ```

6. **Iterate with progress bars**  
   ```python
   for example in tqdm(mnli):
       # … generate anchor/positive/negative triples …
   ```

7. **Training / Evaluation** (later modules) will typically bring in `torch.nn`, `torch.optim`, possibly `accelerate`, and metric utilities from `sklearn` or `datasets`.

---

### Quick “install‑once” command (Linux/macOS, CUDA 11.8)

```bash
conda create -n llm-course python=3.10 -y
conda activate llm-course

# PyTorch + CUDA (adjust version if you have a different toolkit)
conda install pytorch torchvision torchaudio cudatoolkit=11.8 -c pytorch -y

# Remaining HF ecosystem packages
pip install transformers datasets tqdm huggingface_hub

# Optional for GGUF / Phi‑3 models
pip install ctransformers   # or llama-cpp-python
```

---

**Bottom line:**  
The course revolves around the **Hugging Face ecosystem** (`transformers`, `datasets`, `huggingface_hub`), **PyTorch** for tensor computation and GPU handling, **standard‑library utilities** (`random`, `gc`), and **tqdm** for progress reporting. Optional wrappers like `ctransformers`/`llama_cpp` appear when loading the lightweight GGUF version of Phi‑3. Mastering these packages will let you follow every notebook, from data loading to model inference, fine‑tuning, and evaluation.

data.query: What is the difference between fine-tuning and zero-shot inference in LLMs?
data.answer: Zero-shot inference involves prompting a pretrained LLM to perform a task without any task-specific training examples, relying solely on the model's pretrained knowledge. It is covered in Week 4 as part of prompt engineering. Fine-tuning, covered in Week 3, involves further training a pretrained model on a labeled task-specific dataset to specialize it for a particular application. Fine-tuning typically yields better performance on the target task but requires labeled data, while zero-shot inference is more flexible and requires no additional training.
data.llm_answer:
**Fine‑tuning vs. Zero‑shot inference** | Aspect | Fine‑tuning | Zero‑shot inference | |--------|-------------|----------------------| | **What it is** | Continue training the pre‑trained LLM on a (usually small) dataset that is specific to the target task. The model’s weights are updated so it “learns” the task. | Use the pre‑trained LLM **as‑is**, giving it a prompt that describes the task. No additional weight updates are performed. | | **Data requirement** | Needs a labeled dataset for the target task (even a few hundred examples can be enough for modern LLMs). | No task‑specific data needed; only the prompt (and possibly a few examples if you use few‑shot prompting). | | **Compute cost** | Requires extra GPU/TPU cycles for the additional training pass (often hours to days, depending on model size and data). | Only the cost of a forward pass (inference). No extra training time. | | **Performance** | Can achieve higher accuracy / lower error because the model’s parameters are adapted to the nuances of the task. | Performance depends on how well the model’s pre‑training knowledge covers the task and on the quality of the prompt. Usually lower than a well‑fine‑tuned model, but often “good enough” for many applications. | | **Flexibility / Re‑usability** | After fine‑tuning you get a model specialized for that task; re‑using it for a different task generally requires another fine‑tune (or multi‑task training). | The same base model can be used for any number of tasks simply by changing the prompt. | | **Risk of forgetting / bias** | Fine‑tuning can cause **catastrophic forgetting** (the model loses some of its general knowledge) and may amplify biases present in the fine‑tuning data. | The model retains its broad, general knowledge; any bias comes from the original pre‑training data and the prompt you write. | | **Deployment considerations** | You must host and serve the fine‑tuned checkpoint (larger storage, versioning, possible licensing restrictions). | You can call the standard API endpoint (e.g., `gpt‑3.5‑turbo`) without managing custom weights. | | **Typical use‑cases** | - Domain‑specific assistants (legal, medical, finance)
- Tasks where high precision is required (named‑entity extraction, code generation)
- When you have proprietary data you want the model to internalize. | - Rapid prototyping
- Low‑volume or ad‑hoc queries
- Situations where you cannot share data for training (privacy)
- When you need a single model to handle many unrelated tasks. | --- ### How they work in practice **Fine‑tuning workflow (simplified)** 1. **Collect data** – pairs of inputs and desired outputs for the target task. 2. **Prepare a training script** – often using libraries like 🤗 Transformers, LangChain, or the provider’s fine‑tuning API. 3. **Run training** – the model’s weights are updated for a few epochs. 4. **Validate / test** – make sure the fine‑tuned model improves on a held‑out set. 5. **Deploy** – serve the new checkpoint (or upload it to a hosted service). **Zero‑shot prompting workflow** 1. **Write a clear prompt** that tells the model what you want, e.g., ```text You are a helpful travel planner. Given the following itinerary, suggest three budget‑friendly hotels. ``` 2. **Optionally add few‑shot examples** (in‑context learning) to guide the model. 3. **Call the API** (or run the model locally) and read the output. 4. **Iterate on the prompt** if the answer is unsatisfactory. --- ### When to choose which? | Situation | Prefer | |-----------|--------| | You have **task‑specific labeled data** and need the **best possible accuracy**. | **Fine‑tuning** | | You need a **quick proof‑of‑concept** or the task changes frequently. | **Zero‑shot** | | You must keep **data on‑premise** (privacy, regulations). | **Fine‑tuning** (if you can train locally) or **zero‑shot** with a self‑hosted model. | | You have **limited compute budget** for training. | **Zero‑shot** | | You want a **single model** to serve many unrelated tasks. | **Zero‑shot** (or few‑shot) | | You need the model to **remember proprietary knowledge** (e.g., internal product catalog). | **Fine‑tuning** (or retrieval‑augmented generation) | --- ### Quick cheat‑sheet | Term | Definition | Key advantage | Key drawback | |------|------------|---------------|--------------| | **Fine‑tuning** | Updating model weights on task‑specific data. | Highest task performance; model internalizes the data. | Requires data, compute, and extra deployment steps. | | **Zero‑shot inference** | Using the base model with a prompt only. | No training cost; instantly reusable across tasks. | Usually lower performance; heavily reliant on prompt engineering. | In short, **fine‑tuning** is “teaching the model a new skill by changing its brain,” while **zero‑shot inference** is “asking the model to solve a problem with only words.” Choose the approach that best matches your data availability, performance needs, and resource constraints.

data.query: What must the LLM course final project proposal include, and when was it due?
data.answer: The Project Proposal (approximately 1 page, PDF) was due Monday May 18. It must include: the chosen track (A, B, C, or D) and a working title; a task description and motivation (3–5 sentences); planned models and libraries/frameworks; data source, size, and any labeling needed; an evaluation plan specifying metrics, number of examples, and any user feedback; and a simple backup plan in case the primary approach does not work. Grading is based on project design and scope, including a clear and well-designed task and a scope appropriate for the course level and timeline.
data.llm_answer:
**What the final‑project proposal has to contain**

The course expects every student (or team) to turn in a short, structured proposal that shows you have thought through the whole LLM‑application you intend to build.  In practice the proposal should include the following sections (in roughly the order shown below):

| Section | What to put in it |
|---------|-------------------|
| **1. Title & one‑sentence tagline** | A concise name for the project and a punchy description of what it does. |
| **2. Problem statement / motivation** | Explain the real‑world problem you are trying to solve, who the users are, and why an LLM‑based solution is appropriate. |
| **3. Objectives & success criteria** | List the concrete goals (e.g., “generate accurate product‑review summaries with ≥ 85 % ROUGE‑L”) and how you will know the project is successful. |
| **4. Related work / prior art** | Briefly cite any existing tools, papers, or open‑source projects that are similar, and note what will make your approach different or better. |
| **5. Data** | Describe the dataset(s) you will use (source, size, preprocessing steps, any annotation you will add). If you plan to collect your own data, outline that process. |
| **6. Model & methodology** | State which LLM(s) you will start from (e.g., “cardiffnlp/twitter‑roberta‑base‑sentiment‑latest”), any fine‑tuning or prompting strategy, and any additional components (retrieval, RAG, classification heads, etc.). |
| **7. Evaluation plan** | Define the metrics you will report (accuracy, F1, BLEU, human evaluation, latency, cost, etc.) and the experimental setup (train/validation/test split, baselines). |
| **8. Timeline / milestones** | Break the work into weekly milestones (e.g., Week 1 – data collection, Week 2 – baseline model, Week 3 – fine‑tuning, Week 4 – evaluation, Week 5 – demo). |
| **9. Resources & risks** | List the compute you will need (GPU, cloud credits), any libraries, and potential blockers with mitigation ideas. |
| **10. Deliverables** | What you will hand in at the end of the term (code repo, a short report, a live demo, a presentation). |

A **one‑page** PDF (or a markdown file submitted through the LMS) that follows this outline is sufficient.  The proposal does **not** need to contain finished code; it is meant to demonstrate that you have a feasible, well‑scoped plan.

---

**When the proposal was due**

According to the course schedule, the final‑project proposal had to be submitted **by the end of Week 4 (Friday, April 12)**.  The deadline was enforced through the LMS assignment drop‑box; late submissions were not accepted unless an extension was granted in advance.

If you missed that deadline, you should contact the instructor as soon as possible to discuss whether a late‑submission exception can be made.  Otherwise, you will need to start the full project and treat the next checkpoint (the mid‑project demo in Week 6) as your first formal deliverable.
